title: Team
description: One day three best friends Petya, Vasya and Tonya decided to form a team and take part in programming contests. They decided that they would participate in a contest only if at least two of them were sure about the solution. Each of them can either be sure (1) or unsure (0) about the solution to a particular problem. Given n problems and their confidence for each, determine how many problems the team will solve.
input_spec: The first line contains a single integer n (1 <= n <= 1000). Each of the next n lines contains three integers, each either 0 or 1, representing the confidence of Petya, Vasya and Tonya respectively.
output_spec: Print a single integer -- the number of problems the team will solve.

routes.name: Sum threshold
routes.description: For each problem, sum the three confidence values and check if the sum is at least 2.

observations.title: At least two must be sure
observations.description: The team participates if at least 2 out of 3 friends are confident. Since each value is 0 or 1, this means the sum of the three values must be >= 2.

hints.nudge: What is a quick way to check if 'at least two out of three' are true?
hints.question: If you add up three binary values, what sum corresponds to 'at least two are 1'?
hints.formal: For each problem with values (a, b, c), the team solves it if a + b + c >= 2.

observations.title: Count the qualifying problems
observations.description: Iterate through all n problems, and for each one where the sum >= 2, increment a counter.

hints.nudge: How do you accumulate a count over multiple problems?
hints.question: What kind of loop structure do you need to process n problems and count the ones that pass the check?
hints.formal: count = 0; for each problem: read a, b, c; if a+b+c >= 2: count += 1. Print count.

observations.title: No edge cases to worry about
observations.description: The constraints are small (n <= 1000), each value is exactly 0 or 1, and there are no tricky edge cases. A straightforward loop is sufficient.

hints.nudge: Are there any boundary conditions or special inputs to handle?
hints.question: What happens when n = 1 and all three values are 0? Does your solution handle it correctly?
hints.formal: When all are 0, sum = 0 < 2, so it's not counted. When n = 0 is not possible per constraints (n >= 1). The solution is robust as-is.
